response:
THE LAW OF MOORE, FORMULATED BY GORDON ECKERT MOORE IN 1965, PREDICTS THAT THE NUMBER OF TRANSISTORS ON A MICROPROCESSOR WILL DOUBLE APPROXIMATELY EVERY TWO YEARS. THIS LAW HAS BEEN A DRIVING FORCE BEHIND THE RAPID ADVANCEMENT OF COMPUTING TECHNOLOGY FOR DECADES. HOWEVER, AS WE APPROACH THE PHYSICAL LIMITS OF SILICON BASED TECHNOLOGY, IT BECOMES CLEAR THAT THE LAW MAY NOT CONTINUE IN ITS CURRENT FORM FOREVER. SCIENTISTS AND ENGINEERS ARE NOW EXPLORING ALTERNATIVE MATERIALS AND NEW COMPUTING ARCHITECTURES TO SUSTAIN THE GROWTH IN COMPUTING POWER.

***
THE EXACT POINT AT WHICH THE LAW OF MOORE WILL CEASE TO BE VALID IS DIFFICULT TO PREDICT WITH CERTAINTY. FACTORS SUCH AS HEAT DISSIPATION, COST, AND PHYSICAL CONSTRAINTS ARE BECOMING INCREASINGLY CHALLENGING TO OVERCOME. WHILE SOME BELIEVE THE LAW MAY STILL HOLD FOR A FEW MORE YEARS, OTHERS ARGUE THAT IT MAY HAVE ALREADY REACHED ITS LIMITS. IN ANY CASE, THE FUTURE OF COMPUTING WILL LIKELY INVOLVE NEW TECHNOLOGIES AND INNOVATIONS THAT GO BEYOND THE SIMPLE DOUBLING OF TRANSISTORS. DO
